speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 5 with Elizabeth Gorski. You were talking about the fact that you had completed your final year of high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I lived in my aunt's apartment. The war had ended. My mother was not there. We had no news whatsoever. My aunt had had quite a big nice apartment in the center of Lodz. In this time, it was like the Bourke Street Mall. There were continuous strangers coming and going. We slept two or three to a bed. There was an incredible amount of people going through. I found myself in need of some money. I had nothing. I had nothing to wear. Spring was coming. I went and offered the Red Cross my blood-- they were paying some money for it then-- so I could buy myself a summer dress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That must have been the end of May. One day, the bell rings. The doorbell rings. I open the door. There's my mother, looking like a million dollars. And behind her, the caretaker with a big suitcase. What happens was she went to this transit camp. She was sent out of Warsaw with the whole population. She went through the transit camp in Proszkow. She was sent from there as slave labor to a town in Germany called Gorlitz. It is now in the Polish part of the recovered parts. It's called Zgorzelec. Then it what was called Gorlitz. And she was there employed in a train depot, cleaning the trains. Now, Gorlitz had a quantity of fruit orchards around. And there were huge stocks of apples. And even though she was there throughout winter and spring, there were plenty of apples to bite into, so she was never really hungry. And obviously, she looked well from all those apples.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when the Russian army came and took over Gorlitz, she wanted to go home to look for me. She knew nothing about me. But how was she to go? There were no trains. She was a not-so-young lady. She had nothing. What to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, in the street, a Russian army captain bumps into her and hugs her and says, Herr Markson. She'd been in Kharkiv University with him years earlier, and he recognized her. She told him her story. And he said, just you wait. Went off and came back with a horse and buggy, with a bicycle for me, with two suitcases of clothes. And he said, now you go home. And she went off in the buggy. On the way, she lost the-- she lost the horse and buggy. She lost the bicycle. She had to leave one of the suitcases. But she managed to somewhere further get onto a train with one suitcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how she came to lose the horse and buggy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was taken from her by-- by marauding soldiers or whoever it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So she came to Lodz, looking fine, with her suitcase full of clothes. And she stayed there in Anna's flat with me for a while. She started working in Lodz, and so did I. As soon as I finished school, I found a job as proofreader in the daily newspaper. I worked in the printing house at night. And it turned out that I had a good eye as proofreader. You've got to have that sort of inborn attitude for that. I do even now quite a bit of proofreading for the things that Margot writes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I'd finish work at about 2:00 or 3:00 in the morning. We still had curfew then. There were no trains until 5:00 in the morning. And it was quite far from where I lived. And I was afraid to walk the street by myself, so when I'd finish work, I would lie down on the workbench and go to sleep for a while. And one night, on this-- not on the paper I was working on, but another one, an Army paper, there was this soldier working on it and in a uniform. And one night, he had another man with him. And this other man saw me laying down on the workbench. And he told the soldier, bring my coat and cover her. That was Stefan. That's how we met. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told this story my kids later, and they all thought it was awfully romantic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was mid-1945. I worked like that for some months. I-- I enrolled at the university. I started some studies. But these were times of intense disquiet. We were all-- we were not really displaced persons. But we all felt like we-- we couldn't find our place in the world. We didn't know what we wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still on your concealed identity, or were you using your real name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still on my concealed identity. I felt [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very strongly reluctant to go back to my Jewish name in Poland. I had reservations. I just couldn't-- I knew I should, but I couldn't force myself to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any anti-Semitic events that were happening then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not then. No, I was not. But I had a sort of inner resistance to revealing my identity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Early 1946, I decided to go to France. It was a time when everybody was just travelling throughout Europe and in all directions. People were being repatriated from Germany to Poland to Russia. People were traveling from the east to the west. There were borders. There were-- there was army and police everywhere. But we were still traveling. We still managed to do it. I got in touch with my uncle in France. And he said, come. You can stay with us here, [INAUDIBLE]. So I told my mother I'm going to France. Mother wasn't very happy about it, but she couldn't really stop me. She had, in the meantime, met someone who told us that he knew my father and my brother in Wilno, that they were shipped to a concentration camp in Stutthoff, wherever that was, somewhere on the Baltic Sea, that he knew that my father had been killed there. He didn't know-- he wasn't sure about my brother, that my brother had given his identity as half-Jewish. He couldn't say he was fully Aryan, because he was with a father who was Jewish. And my mother understood from there that he had been killed also. And I don't think it ever-- it ever reconciled herself to the fact that she'd let him go. She felt she could have saved him if he was there with us. Anyway, she let me go, unhappily, but she did let me go. She gave me $50 that she must have left from the money that had been distributed around during the occupation. I met in Lodz a young man. His name was Abrasza Zemsz, who came from Paris on the repatriation train. It was a train shuttling through Europe from east to west and from west to east, carrying refugees wherever they wanted to go. And they brought a number of people from Germany back to Poland. Then they were going back to Germany. And something like, oh, a dozen people loaded onto the train to go back with them, to go to France, to go to the west. And I was one of them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember how we parlayed our way through the Polish border. But somehow or else, we did, under the pretense that we were the nursing staff to look after the refugees. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were just sort of empty train wagons with just little folding beds, sort of two pieces of timber and a piece of denim in between. We went through Czechoslovakia. We stopped for a day in Prague. It was a very beautiful experience for me, because I had never been outside Poland's borders before. We went through Germany. We stopped on the French border in a DP's camp, a camp for Displaced Persons. And there we were told, not going any further, not going to France. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went to a beg the camp commander. I told him I had an uncle in France and he wanted me to go to his place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took my uncle's name and address, Uncle lived then in Marseilles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he sent him a wire, asking whether it was true that he wanted me to stay with him. And Uncle confirmed that, so I was allowed to leave and I was put on a train to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a day's break, waiting for a nice night train to Marseilles. I don't remember where I had the money to pay for tickets, whether there were any tickets. What I remember was that I was in Paris and I was hungry and I couldn't buy any food. There were stalls with beautiful sandwiches on pieces of French sticks in all metro stations or in the streets. But you had to have coupons to buy them. And I didn't have any coupons. I was still a very illegal citizen. So I ended by-- I ended up buying a pound of fresh dates. And that filled me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to my uncle. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was around March, '46. I enrolled in school to do French matric. And my uncle thought that I was preposterous. How can I do French matric in three months when his daughter has to work all year to school? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And actually, I did it. I guess these were times where war victims had special concessions. But anyhow, I did it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you enroll at?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a-- I think it was a sort of a correspondence course. It was not a-- you know, I didn't have to sit in school. But I did. I did the French matric. It was an external exam. It was like it used to be in Victoria that all the kids had to sit in the exhibition building. We had to sit also in a building where all the schools had sent their students for exams. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the meantime, I had some documents worked out for me, a French identity card. And I had there, without any qualms, reverted to my name, [? Vishov. ?] It didn't worry me at all, so I got documents in this name. And then, I wanted to enroll at university, but my uncle told me, you can't go to university. You have no father to pay for your studies. My daughter can go to university. I can help her. But you can't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got in touch with a friend or distant cousin who was in Paris working as a proofreader in a Polish paper. And she wrote to me, you can come here and get my job, because I'm moving-- I'm being moved to another position. So I went to Paris, and I got a job as proofreader in the Polish paper. And I rented a very small room in a very cheap hotel on the fifth floor, where it was freezing in winter. There was no heating. The central heating had broken down and nobody could repair it. Well, it was so hot in summer that I would open the door. And there was not really a balcony, but a sort of a very narrow strip enclosed by a railing, and I just put my feet through that railing to have a bit of fresh air. And I worked as a proofreader. And then, after a while, I was asked to learn to work on a linotype, which was the print-setting machine then. They had no Polish-speaking printers. And it was very difficult for French-speaking or Jewish-speaking, Yiddish-speaking-- that was a Jewish print shop-- guys to work on the Polish texts. So they taught me how the-- how the linotype works. And I was doing that. And one day, who walks into the print shop but Stefan. He never told me, never, 'til maybe 40 years later, that he didn't quite accidentally take that assignment to Paris. I thought he did, but he never quite admitted to it. Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here comes the story of my gold bracelet. When my mother was in the transit camp in Proszkow looking for me, walking around and asking people whether they have seen such and such a  girl, this woman that she'd never seen before came to her, took a thick gold chain from her hand, and gave it to my mother. Take this, you may need some money to buy back your daughter from wherever she is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this woman a stranger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Total stranger. Mother had never seen her before or after. The chain was very thick and heavy, and it had no special arts work. It was just obviously made to preserve as much gold as possible. When I was going to France, mother gave me the chain. She said, I think it belongs to you. I got it to find you, so you may as well take it. And I wore it every day. I never wanted to leave it in the hotel room. I wore it on my hand to work and everywhere. But I had to take it off to wash my hands. They were black from working on those printing machines. And one night, I forgot and I left it on the sink in the print shop. And it was-- of course, it wasn't there the next morning when I arrived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Months later, we were, Stefan and I, invited to a function in the Polish embassy. And I saw my gold chain on the ambassador's wife chain-- hand. We couldn't say a thing. We couldn't prove that it was mine. It had no visible signs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she had just probably bought it through some black market connections. So there it went. Well, that's my life in France, just about. We got married in Paris in '48 and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married by the mayor, which is a mayor of the-- of the area Paris 20s, where we lived. And we had a very small reception, just a dinner for us offered by some friends of Stefan's that I'd never known before. His brother and sister-in-law, who were then in Paris waiting for permits to come to Australia, were there with us. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We were then living in a very small hotel room in the Rue de Pyrenees. There was hardly space for a bed and a single gas burner and a tiny table. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A floor below in the same hotel lived his brother and sister-in-law and five-year-old son, Victor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when I moved in with Stefan, well, he was working on the Polish paper. I was working in the print shop. We were hardly ever home. But on Sunday mornings, they would send Victor up to our room five, six times during the morning, just to tease us. We stayed there for some months. I got pregnant very quickly. That was a time when we all wanted to make up for the lost time and make up for the lost lives. We wanted to create a family. We wanted to have children. I was dreaming of 12 then. Thank God I stopped at three. So I very quickly became pregnant. And then, I wanted to be with my mother. Mother didn't want to leave Poland, so we went back. Anyway, Stefan's assignment was coming to an end. His brother wanted us to come to Australia with him, but I was reluctant. We went back to Poland and we settled in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying before that people were anxious, and so were you, to have children to make up for the loss. Did you develop any Jewish affinity then?

speaker: interviewee
text: To some extent. To some extent. I was amongst Jewish kids a lot in Paris. After a while, after being on my own in that little hotel room, I'd become sick of it. And I found a home for Jewish girls, sort of late teens and early 20s, where there was, oh, some maybe 20 girls from-- Jewish girls from all parts of the world. I remember a very lovely Russian girl, an Austrian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was best friends with a French girl from Alsace. A Greek Jewish girl. We were paying some very small fee for being there. And we all had some jobs or others. And we felt sort of more secure living there. I also went, a couple of times, to a Jewish holiday camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A number of my friends then were going to Israel and wanted to take me with them. But somehow, I did not have that-- did not feel that need to go there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And anyway, my mother was still in Poland and I did not want to be too far away, so we ended up going back. And then we only left for Australia some years later, in '62. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What else is there of interest to say? Can you tell me about the years between coming to Australia and after leaving Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were, in one sense, quite rewarding years. We were all sort of working at rebuilding the country that was completely ruined.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a small flat. He got a small flat. He was-- Stefan was a journalist. He was working for the newspaper. I wasn't working. I was pregnant. We moved into the flat about two weeks before our first daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city was the flat in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't work then. But I went to work as soon as my daughter was a few months old. We took a nanny, a young country girl as a nanny, and I went to work at just some sort of a clerical job.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born on the 1st January, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you call her?

speaker: interviewee
text: We called her Joanna. But I wanted her to be called Kay, so I kept calling her Kay. And that's what she became. That's what everybody-- she actually changed her name in her documents later here. Then, a year later, I had a son. And then, I went to study [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] arts, with specializing in journalism and history of the Polish press. So I had two kids. I had a night job in a weekly magazine, proofreading. And I was studying. I completed the studies in '52, something like that, '53.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother living at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She moved to Warsaw. She got qualified after the war as a librarian. And she worked in a library in Warsaw. She loved the job. She was really-- for the first time in her life, she was totally independent. She was not mother and housewife anymore. She was an independent woman with her own interests.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see much of her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, she lived with us for a while. Then she moved with a friend of hers who was living just about next door to us. So we saw a lot of her. She was always very, very helpful to me in bringing up the children and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your mother exchange stories of what had happened through the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were never great for words between us. I always felt some reluctance to talk to Mother about war things. I grew with a feeling-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it might have been totally false. But I sort of went into life with the feeling that she would have preferred, if she had to lose one, to have kept my brother rather than me, because he was always her closest. I was my father's. He was hers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did not-- we were not really on very close terms. I mean, we did what had to be done for each other, but we were not very close. But when we decided to come to Australia, Stefan had two brothers here, and things in Poland were becoming unpleasant. In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the-- you know, the feeling about Jews was getting unpleasant. And we decided-- and there was also a very great fear of war in Europe. The Soviet Union was arming heavily. And from a friend who was in the-- in some high position in the army, we heard of plans that, in case of war, Poland would become the area of total destruction, to allow the Western armies into the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided to come to Australia. My mother was very unhappy about the decision. She came with us. She wouldn't have stayed on her own. She'd come with us. What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '62. We were-- oh, we were trying for visas and passports for-- it's taken us four years to get all the documents. We've started in '58. We completed our papers and left in February '62.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to stop you for a tape break.
